questions.question: Which statement best describes an eating disorder?
options: A lifestyle choice made out of vanity about appearance
A passing phase of fussy eating that people grow out of on their own
A serious, treatable mental illness that a person cannot simply choose to stop
A diet that has gone only slightly too far

questions.question: Eating behaviours such as restricting, bingeing or purging are usually best understood as:
options: A bid to gain attention from other people
A way of coping with difficult thoughts and feelings that feel out of control
An attempt to improve appearance and fitness
A way of punishing family members

questions.question: What does the course say about the causes of eating disorders?
options: They are caused solely by the media
There is no single cause; a mix of genetic, psychological and social factors combine differently in each person
They are caused simply by a lack of willpower
There is one clear cause in every case

questions.question: When someone develops an eating disorder, who is it helpful to blame?
options: Their family
No one; looking for someone to blame is never helpful
The person themselves
Whoever introduced them to dieting

questions.question: Who can be affected by an eating disorder?
options: Only people who are visibly underweight
Only young women
People of any age, gender, ethnicity, body shape and background
Only teenagers

questions.question: What is your role as a care worker in relation to eating disorders?
options: To assess and treat the person yourself
To diagnose the specific type of eating disorder
To decide whether the person really needs any help
To notice changes, offer kind support and raise concerns so professional help can follow

questions.question: Anorexia is characterised by:
options: Regular binges that are never followed by purging
Avoiding foods because of their taste, smell or texture
Eating large amounts and always feeling in control
Severely limiting food, an intense fear of gaining weight and a distorted view of the body

questions.question: Bulimia typically involves:
options: Only ever restricting food and never bingeing
Avoiding food because of a fear of choking
Eating slowly and never feeling out of control
Eating a large amount in a short time and then trying to undo it by vomiting, laxatives, fasting or over-exercising

questions.question: Which statement about binge eating disorder is correct?
options: It is simply overeating and a lack of willpower
It is the most common eating disorder, and the binges are not followed by purging
It only affects people who are overweight
The binges are always followed by purging

questions.question: What does OSFED stand for?
options: Occasional Selective Food Eating Difficulty
Ongoing Selective Emotional Dieting
Overeating and Severe Fasting Eating Disorder
Other Specified Feeding or Eating Disorder

questions.question: OSFED is best described as:
options: Not really an eating disorder at all
A condition only ever diagnosed in children
Common and just as serious as other eating disorders
A mild, lesser problem than other eating disorders

questions.question: What does ARFID stand for?
options: Avoidant or Restrictive Food Intake Disorder
Anorexia-Related Food Intake Difficulty
Avoidant Reaction to Fatty Ingredient Diets
Anxious Refusal of Food In Daytime

questions.question: Unlike the other eating disorders, ARFID is:
options: Always about wanting to lose weight
Not driven by concerns about weight or body shape
Only ever found in adults
Never harmful to a person's health

questions.question: Which of these is a behavioural warning sign that someone may be struggling?
options: Looking forward to sharing meals with others
Feeling warm and comfortable all the time
Suddenly having noticeably stronger teeth
Becoming secretive around food or developing strict eating rituals

questions.question: Which of these is a physical sign that might be linked to an eating disorder?
options: A steady, untroubled appetite
Feeling cold, tiredness, dizziness, hair thinning or a sore throat from vomiting
Increased warmth and boundless energy
Stronger teeth and thicker hair

questions.question: No single sign proves that someone has an eating disorder, so what matters most is:
options: Keeping your observations to yourself
Noticing a pattern, or a change from how the person usually is, and reporting what you have seen
Waiting until you are completely certain of the diagnosis
Ignoring it unless the person looks underweight

questions.question: One of the most damaging myths about eating disorders is that:
options: Recovery is genuinely possible
They are serious mental illnesses
They can affect people of any gender
You can tell whether someone has one just by looking at them

questions.question: Which people are especially likely to be overlooked because they do not fit the stereotype?
options: Only very young children
Men, older people and those from minority communities
No one is ever overlooked
Young, thin women

questions.question: Which statement about recovery is true?
options: Eating disorders are lifelong and untreatable
Only a very few people ever improve
Recovery is impossible without medication
Eating disorders are treatable, and many people make a full recovery with the right support

questions.question: Eating disorders can damage physical health because:
options: They only cause mild tiredness
They only ever affect the stomach
They have no real physical effects
The body can be starved of the energy and nutrients it needs, or damaged by repeated purging, and almost every system can be affected

questions.question: Purging and restriction can upset the balance of essential salts such as potassium, which can:
options: Disturb the heart's rhythm and, in the worst cases, cause it to stop
Actually improve how the heart works
Cause only a mild, brief headache
Have no effect on the body at all

questions.question: Compared with other mental illnesses, eating disorders carry:
options: A very low risk to life
A risk only to older people
No risk to life at all
One of the highest risks to life, from physical complications and, tragically, from suicide

questions.question: If someone shows signs of physical distress such as fainting, chest pain or severe weakness, you should:
options: Wait quietly to see whether it passes
Say nothing to anyone and let them rest
Seek urgent medical help, following your organisation's procedures
Offer them a large meal to build their strength

questions.question: National guidance for professionals says that body weight on its own:
options: Always shows exactly how ill someone is
Should not be used alone to decide how serious an eating disorder is or whether someone needs help
Is the only measure that really matters
Can be ignored completely in every case

questions.question: What is Beat?
options: A recommended mealtime routine
A specific type of eating disorder
A specialist hospital ward for eating disorders
The UK's eating disorder charity, which runs a helpline and offers information and support

questions.question: An eating disorder is a lifestyle choice that a person can simply decide to stop.
options: True
False

questions.question: No one chooses to have an eating disorder.
options: True
False

questions.question: Eating disorders only affect young women, so you need not consider them in men or older people.
options: True
False

questions.question: You can usually tell whether someone has an eating disorder just by looking at them.
options: True
False

questions.question: A person can be seriously, even critically, unwell with an eating disorder while being a normal weight or above.
options: True
False

questions.question: ARFID is driven by a person's concerns about their weight and body shape.
options: True
False

questions.question: Getting specialist help early gives someone the best chance of a full recovery.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is a good idea to praise someone with an eating disorder for looking well or healthier.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of these are recognised eating disorders described in the course? (Select all that apply)
options: Anorexia
Binge eating disorder
Bulimia
A seasonal eating habit
Occasional social dieting

questions.question: Which of the following are behavioural warning signs? (Select all that apply)
options: Feeling warm and full of energy
Becoming secretive around food
Happily enjoying shared family meals
Strict rules and rituals, such as cutting food into tiny pieces
Avoiding meals or eating with others

questions.question: Which of the following are physical signs that might be linked to an eating disorder? (Select all that apply)
options: Noticeably improved eyesight
Increased muscle strength
Dizziness
Hair thinning
Feeling cold

questions.question: Which of the following are emotional or psychological signs? (Select all that apply)
options: A preoccupation with food, weight or body shape
A relaxed, untroubled attitude to eating
Anxiety and irritability
Consistently high self-esteem
Low mood

questions.question: When supporting someone at mealtimes, what should you do? (Select all that apply)
options: Draw attention to exactly how much they are eating
Keep the atmosphere calm and unhurried
Offer gentle, steady encouragement without pressure
Follow the person's care plan
Force or coerce the person to eat

questions.question: Which of these should you try to avoid when supporting someone with an eating disorder? (Select all that apply)
options: Talking about diets, calories or your own eating in front of them
Comments about their weight, size or appearance
Listening without judgement
Taking their feelings seriously
Comments about how much or how little they are eating

questions.question: When you are worried that someone may have an eating disorder, which actions are appropriate? (Select all that apply)
options: Wait several weeks to see whether it goes away on its own
Diagnose and start treating the eating disorder yourself
Report your concerns to a senior or manager, following your organisation's procedures
Record what you have observed, clearly and factually
Point the person and their family towards Beat

questions.question: A person can be seriously unwell with an eating disorder at any ___.
___ is the UK's eating disorder charity, which runs a helpline and offers information and support.
The usual route to specialist help is the person's ___, who can assess them and refer them to specialist eating disorder services.
OSFED is common and just as ___ as anorexia, bulimia or binge eating disorder.
At mealtimes, follow the person's care ___, which may set out exactly how to offer food and drink and how much support they need.
